model_narrative: We run a network of canning plants that ship product to a set of markets. We decide how many cases to ship from each plant to each market. Every plant-to-market lane carries a transport cost per case that depends on how far apart the plant and market are. Our goal is to make the total transportation cost over all shipments as small as possible.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the canning plants that ship product out

params.name: a
params.doc: the production capacity of each plant, in cases; a plant cannot ship out more than this

params.name: b
params.doc: the demand at each market, in cases; each market must receive at least this much

params.name: d
params.doc: the distance from each plant to each market, in thousands of miles

params.name: f
params.doc: the freight rate, in dollars per case per thousand miles

params.name: c
params.doc: the transport cost per case shipped from a plant to a market, in thousands of dollars per case; derived from the freight rate and the distance

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the quantity shipped from each plant to each market, in cases

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total transportation cost over all shipments, in thousands of dollars

constraints.name: z
constraints.intent: The total transportation cost gathers the cost of every shipment across the whole network. Value each plant-to-market shipment at its per-case transport cost, add these up over all plant and market combinations, and set the total transportation cost equal to that sum.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Each plant can only ship out as much as it can produce. For each plant, the total quantity shipped from that plant to all markets must not exceed that plant's capacity.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Every market must have its demand met. For each market, the total quantity shipped into that market from all plants must be at least that market's demand.

query: We're looking at routing more of San Diego's output toward the two inland lines together — we'd like its combined shipments into the Chicago and Topeka assembly lines to come to at least 450 loads. What does the optimizer return for cost and the lane plan?

query: There's a proposal on the table to share the Topeka line across both plants instead of leaning on one — San Diego's shipments to Topeka shouldn't be more than half of everything that reaches the Topeka line, counting Seattle's and San Diego's loads to Topeka together. Plug it in and walk me through the cost.

query: We're evaluating a two-part rebalancing package: trim Seattle's combined output to the New York and Chicago lines down to 250 loads or under, and at the same time pull San Diego's combined shipments into the Chicago and Topeka lines up to at least 400 loads. Run the pair together and tell me where the logistics bill lands.
expected_pyomo:
model.sea_west_cap = Constraint(expr=model.x['seattle', 'new-york'] + model.x['seattle', 'chicago'] <= 250)
model.sd_inland_min = Constraint(expr=model.x['san-diego', 'chicago'] + model.x['san-diego', 'topeka'] >= 400)

query: Seattle dumps almost everything into one or two lines and barely touches the rest, which leaves us exposed if a lane goes down. Shouldn't Seattle be carrying a real presence on every assembly line — say at least 80 loads to each of them? What's keeping the model from spreading it out?

query: Some lanes are carrying really heavy loads. Wouldn't capping every plant-to-line lane at 200 give us a more balanced, resilient network? What's the model seeing that I'm not?

query: The whole New York line is being fed by the far plant while Seattle, which is right there, barely shows up on it. Shouldn't Seattle at least match San Diego on the New York lane rather than letting the distant plant carry it? What's the model seeing that I'm not?

query: A few lanes look maxed out. Couldn't we just hold every lane to 275 loads and still cover everyone? What's the tradeoff I'm missing?
